name: Long Dragon
description: The Long Dragon, or simply Dragon (龙, lóng), is one of the most powerful and revered creatures in Chinese mythology. It is a symbol of power, strength, good luck, and prosperity. Unlike the often malevolent dragons of Western lore, Chinese dragons are generally benevolent and associated with control over water, rainfall, typhoons, and floods. They are also linked to the emperor and are considered auspicious symbols of imperial authority. The Long Dragon is often depicted with a serpentine body, four legs with sharp claws, and a long, whiskered face. They possess immense magical abilities and are capable of shapeshifting and controlling the elements. The Long Dragon's influence permeates all aspects of Chinese culture, from art and architecture to literature and folklore. They are seen as guardians and protectors, ensuring harmony and balance in the world. Their presence is invoked in ceremonies and festivals to bring good fortune and ward off evil. The Long Dragon is considered the ultimate symbol of yang, the masculine principle, and represents the creative forces of the universe. Respect and reverence for the Long Dragon are deeply ingrained in Chinese society, reflecting its enduring significance as a cultural icon.
subtitle: Symbol of power and good fortune
appearance: The Long Dragon typically possesses a long, serpentine body covered in scales, often iridescent green or blue. It has four legs with sharp claws, a long, whiskered face with prominent eyes, and a flowing mane. It may have antlers similar to a stag and barbels around its mouth. The number of toes on its feet was once restricted to imperial dragons, common dragons usually having four toes. They often carry a flaming pearl, a symbol of wisdom and perfection.
habitat: Long Dragons are typically associated with bodies of water, such as rivers, lakes, and oceans. They also inhabit mountains and caves, particularly those with access to water sources. Some dragons are said to reside in the celestial realm, controlling the weather and ensuring rainfall.
behavior: Long Dragons are generally benevolent and wise creatures, though their temperament can vary. They are considered powerful guardians and protectors, ensuring balance and harmony in the world. They are often invoked in ceremonies and festivals to bring good fortune and ward off evil. While generally benevolent, they can be fierce when angered or provoked, unleashing their elemental powers to defend their territory or punish wrongdoers.

keyMyths.title: The Dragon King of the East Sea
keyMyths.description: Ao Guang, the Dragon King of the East Sea, controls the waters and weather of the East Sea. He is often depicted as a powerful and benevolent ruler, but can also be demanding and quick to anger, requiring offerings and respect from humans.

keyMyths.title: Nüwa and the Dragon
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the creation myth, the goddess Nüwa defeats a black dragon that was causing chaos and flooding. She then uses the dragon's body to patch up the sky and restore order to the world, showcasing the dragon's destructive potential but also its ability to be used for good.

sources.author: E.T.C. Werner
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends, including detailed descriptions of dragons and their roles in Chinese culture.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends, including detailed descriptions of dragons and their roles in Chinese culture.

sources.author: Lihui Yang
sources.description: Provides an overview of various aspects of Chinese mythology, including the symbolism and significance of the Long Dragon.
